ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised pork leg rice; FOODON_00002372 (pork dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific braised pork leg with rice composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for braised pork leg rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of braised pork leg (roughly 150 g meat and skin), a bowl of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g cooked), a hard-boiled egg, and braising sauce with preserved vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Braised pork leg with rice — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for braised pork (猪脚) and steamed white rice, weighted by typical serving composition
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo / Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists braised pork (pig trotter) and steamed white rice as separate entries. Per-100 g composite values were derived by weighting: approximately 33% braised pork leg (skin-on, braised) and 44% steamed white rice, with residual braising sauce (~23%). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for braised pork leg (NDB 10219) and cooked white rice (NDB 20444). Values represent a plausible central estimate for a typical Singaporean Longjiang pork leg rice plate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed pork-rice composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Longjiang pork leg rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant glycemic driver being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 for plain boiled white rice per University of Sydney GI Database) attenuated by the substantial fat (7.8 g/100 g) and protein (9.2 g/100 g) content of the braised pork and sauce components, which are well-documented to lower the effective GI of mixed rice meals. A composite GI of approximately 54 is consistent with published values for mixed pork-rice meals in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). GL per serving = (54 × 92.25 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 49.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid (~480 mg sodium per 100 g; ~2160 mg per standard 450 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Longjiang pork leg rice due to its very high sodium content (~2160 mg per serving, approaching or exceeding the daily 2000 mg WHO limit in a single meal); requesting reduced sauce or smaller portions is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~50 per serving) driven by white rice, partially offset by fat and protein from braised pork
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the substantial glycemic load (~50 per 450 g serving) from the white rice component; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion while retaining the protein-rich pork can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from pork skin and collagen-rich braised meat, combined with high sodium and moderate cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Longjiang pork leg rice infrequently due to its combination of high sodium (~2160 mg/serving), saturated fat (~12 g/serving from pork skin), and moderate cholesterol (~248 mg/serving), all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Longjiang Pork Leg Rice (隆江猪脚饭, Lóng Jiāng Zhū Jiǎo Fàn) is a popular Cantonese-origin braised pork dish widely served in Singapore and Malaysia. The dish originates from Longjiang town in Guangdong province, China, and features a whole or half pork leg (trotter or knuckle, skin-on) slow-braised in a rich, aromatic sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, rock sugar, five-spice powder, garlic, ginger, and sometimes preserved mustard greens (mei cai). The long braising process renders the pork skin gelatinous and deeply flavoured, while the meat becomes tender and falls off the bone. It is served over steamed white rice, typically accompanied by a hard-boiled egg braised in the same sauce and a portion of the braising liquid poured over the rice. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, and deeply umami, with a glossy, caramelised appearance from the dark soy and rock sugar. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~185 kcal/100 g), moderate in protein (~9.2 g/100 g), and contains meaningful fat (~7.8 g/100 g) largely from pork skin collagen and subcutaneous fat. Sodium content is high (~480 mg/100 g) due to the soy-based braising liquid. The glycemic index of the composite dish is estimated at approximately 54 (low GI), as the fat and protein from the pork attenuate the glycemic response of the white rice. Clinically, the dish is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat load, and requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its glycemic load. It is a non-halal dish as it contains pork.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Longjiang pork leg rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo entries for braised pork (pig trotter) and steamed white rice, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for mixed pork-rice meals. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the weighting rationale is documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on HPB Foodo braised pork and steamed white rice entries weighted by typical serving composition (~33% braised pork, ~44% rice, ~23% braising sauce by mass). 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA source entries for this food type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA source entries for this food type. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entries for this composite dish. 5. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Longjiang pork leg rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 7. Sodium content is notably high and is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. 8. The cuisine tag in the input JSON references the restaurant name rather than a cuisine category; this has been corrected to 'Chinese Singaporean' based on the dish's origin and Singapore context.
